other_pets.what_other_pets_do_you_currently_have_sp:
We currently have two other pets:

1. Dog – 1 mixed-breed rescue dog named "Ruby," female, approximately 6 years old (we adopted her at around 2 years of age from the county shelter in 2021).
2. Cat – 1 domestic short-hair cat named "Milo," male, 4 years old (we adopted him as a kitten in 2021 from a local rescue).

Both pets are fully integrated into our household and are indoor family pets (Ruby goes outside only on leash or in our fenced backyard; Milo is indoor-only).
other_pets.are_these_pets_up_to_date_on_vaccines_an:
yes. Ruby is spayed and fully up to date on all core vaccines (rabies, distemper/parvo, Bordetella). She also receives monthly flea/tick and heartworm prevention. Her last vet wellness exam was on 2025-03-18 at Greenwood Animal Hospital.

Milo is neutered and current on his vaccines (rabies and FVRCP). He is also on a monthly flea prevention regimen. His last wellness visit was on 2025-04-02 at the same clinic. We keep copies of all veterinary records and can provide them upon request.
other_pets.have_you_ever_surrendered_a_pet_if_so_wh: no. I have never surrendered a pet to a shelter or rescue. Any pets I’ve had have stayed with me or my immediate family for their full lives, with the exception of one childhood dog who moved with my parents when I went away to college. I take the decision to adopt very seriously and view it as a long-term, lifelong commitment, so surrendering a pet would only be considered in an extreme situation where the animal’s safety or well-being absolutely required a different home (for example, if a pet developed needs that could not safely be met in my household despite working with vets and behaviorists).
other_pets.have_you_ever_had_a_pet_euthanized_if_so:
yes. When I was in high school, my family’s senior dog, a Labrador mix named "Buddy," was euthanized at age 14 due to severe arthritis, chronic pain, and advanced kidney disease. Over the course of about a year, his mobility declined significantly despite pain medications, joint supplements, and physical therapy. He began having more bad days than good, was struggling to stand, and had started to lose interest in eating and interacting.

After several consultations with our veterinarian and a quality-of-life assessment, we made the decision to euthanize him at the vet’s office so that he would not suffer further. It was extremely difficult emotionally, but I learned a lot about what it means to prioritize an animal’s comfort and dignity at the end of life. That experience has made me very proactive about senior pet care and regular vet checkups.
other_pets.have_you_ever_lost_a_pet_to_an_accident: no. I have not lost a pet to an accident. I grew up with a strong emphasis on safety around animals—keeping dogs leashed or in secure fenced areas, keeping cats indoors, using secure collars and ID tags, and making sure doors and gates are closed properly. Because of that, I am very cautious about supervision, especially in new environments or around open doors, busy streets, or bodies of water. I plan to maintain that same level of vigilance with any new pet I adopt.
other_pets.how_do_you_discipline_correct_your_pets:
My approach to discipline is based on positive reinforcement and redirection, not punishment. I do not use physical punishment, yelling, or any methods that rely on fear or intimidation.

Day to day, this looks like:

- **Positive reinforcement:** Rewarding desired behaviors with treats, praise, or play. For example, when Ruby sits calmly before we cross a street, she gets a "good girl" and sometimes a small treat. When Milo uses his scratching post instead of furniture, he gets praise and occasional treats or play with his favorite wand toy.

- **Redirection:** If an unwanted behavior occurs, I redirect to an appropriate behavior instead of just saying "no." For example, if Ruby starts to jump up on guests, I’ll calmly ask her to "sit" or go to her bed and then reward her when she complies. If Milo scratches the couch, I gently move him away and guide him toward the scratching post, then reward him for using it.

- **Management:** I try to set them up for success so that problem behaviors are less likely in the first place. For example, putting shoes and kids’ toys away so Ruby isn’t tempted to chew them, using baby gates when needed, and having scratching posts in the areas where Milo likes to stretch.

- **Calm, consistent cues:** If I need to interrupt a behavior, I use a firm but calm "uh-uh" or "leave it" and then cue an alternative behavior. Consistency is important, so everyone in the household uses the same words and expectations.

If a behavior issue ever becomes more significant, I would work with a positive-reinforcement-based trainer or behaviorist to develop a structured plan, rather than trying aversive methods on my own.
other_pets.other_reason_to_rehome_return_dog:
The only reasons I consider truly valid for rehoming or returning a dog would be severe safety or welfare concerns that cannot be resolved despite diligent, professional intervention. For example:

- A dog showing repeated, unmanageable aggression toward humans or other pets, where a qualified veterinary behaviorist and trainer have been involved and it’s determined that the dog would be safer in a different type of environment (e.g., no children, experienced handler, or a home without other animals).
- A significant, unexpected change in the adopter’s health or capacity (such as a severe disability or cognitive decline) where the person can no longer physically or mentally care for the dog, and there is no trusted family or friend who can take over the dog’s care.

Outside of these kinds of extreme, safety- or health-related circumstances, I do not see typical training or adjustment issues (chewing, barking, house-training accidents, etc.) as acceptable reasons to give up a dog. Those are issues I expect to work through with patience, training, and, when necessary, professional help.
other_pets.have_you_ever_had_to_seek_out_training_f:
yes. When we adopted Ruby, she had some leash reactivity and anxiety around unfamiliar dogs, likely due to limited socialization before we got her. Within the first month, we enrolled her in a basic obedience class at Pawsitive Steps Training Center, which used only positive reinforcement methods. That class helped us with foundation cues like "sit," "stay," "leave it," and loose-leash walking.

After we noticed that Ruby would bark and lunge a bit when other dogs passed too close on walks, we booked a series of four private sessions with one of their trainers. The trainer showed us how to:
- Increase the distance from triggers (other dogs) to a level where Ruby could stay calm.
- Use high-value treats and a "look at me" cue to help Ruby focus on us instead of the other dog.
- Gradually decrease the distance as she improved, always staying within her comfort zone.

Over about three months of consistent practice (short, daily sessions), Ruby’s reactivity decreased significantly. She can now pass other dogs on the opposite side of the street with minimal reaction, and we know how to manage her space and stress levels. The experience was very positive overall and reinforced my belief in early intervention and professional guidance when behavior issues show up.

I’ve also done a kitten socialization and handling class with Milo when he was young, which helped him become more comfortable with nail trims, being handled by the vet, and being around visitors. That was a shorter, 3-week course but very helpful in building confidence and preventing future issues.
other_pets.are_you_willing_to_invest_in_training_do:
yes. We fully understand that adopting a dog (or any animal) is a long-term financial and time commitment.

- **Training:** We are absolutely willing to enroll in group training classes or private sessions if needed. We already budget for pet expenses, including training, and have used professional trainers in the past. My philosophy is that early training is an investment that pays off in a better-adjusted, happier dog and a more harmonious household.

- **Doggy daycare:** If we adopt a dog who is particularly social and enjoys other dogs, or if we go through a period where our work schedules are unusually demanding, we are open to using a reputable doggy daycare 1–2 times per week. There is a well-reviewed daycare facility (Happy Tails Daycare) about 10 minutes from our home that we have already researched.

- **Dog walker:** On days when one of us cannot get home at lunch or if we ever have a temporary schedule change (e.g., business travel, medical appointments), we are willing to hire a dog walker for mid-day walks or potty breaks. We currently have a local pet sitter/dog walker we’ve used for Ruby when we travel, and we trust her with our pets and home.

In short, we are committed to using whatever combination of training, daycare, and walking support is necessary to ensure the dog’s physical exercise, mental stimulation, and emotional well-being.
other_pets.if_you_are_applying_for_a_puppy_have_you:
yes. While the dog we are applying for now is not a tiny 8-week-old puppy, I do have prior experience raising a dog from a young puppy stage.

When I was in my mid-20s, I adopted a 10-week-old Border Collie mix named "Scout" with my then-roommate. We went through all the classic puppy phases: house-training, crate training, chewing, mouthing, and teaching basic manners. We set up a consistent potty schedule (out every 2–3 hours at first, gradually stretching it), used a crate at night and when unsupervised, and puppy-proofed the apartment by putting shoes, cables, and trash out of reach.

We also attended puppy kindergarten and then a basic obedience course. During teething, we provided lots of appropriate chew toys (frozen Kongs, rubber toys) and redirected him every single time he went for furniture or hands. It was a lot of work and required a ton of consistency and patience, but he grew into a very well-behaved adult dog.

That experience gave me a very realistic understanding of how intense the puppy period can be—sleep interruptions, accidents, and constant supervision—and I know that consistency, positive reinforcement, and routine are key. If we ever adopt another young puppy in the future, we’re prepared to repeat that level of structure and commitment.

about_the_dogcat_you_wish_to_adopt.please_describe_what_drew_you_to_this_pa:
We are specifically interested in adopting "Luna," the 3-year-old medium-sized mixed-breed dog currently in your care. Several things drew us to her:

1. **Her story and background:** In her profile, you mentioned that Luna was surrendered due to her previous owner’s job loss and housing instability, not because of any behavioral issues on her part. That really resonated with us, as we know that many wonderful animals end up in shelters for reasons completely outside their control. We feel strongly about giving a stable, long-term home to a dog who has already had to cope with major changes.

2. **Her temperament description:** Luna is described as affectionate, people-oriented, and eager to please, with a moderate energy level. That combination is exactly what we’ve been looking for. We want a dog who enjoys cuddling on the couch in the evenings but is also happy to go for daily walks, hikes on weekends, and play sessions in the yard. The fact that she is reported to be friendly with other dogs and respectful of cats is particularly important, because we have both a resident dog (Ruby) and a cat (Milo).

3. **Size and age:** Luna’s medium size (around 40–45 lbs) and age (3 years) are ideal for our household. We have the space and time for a dog of her size, and we appreciate that she is past the very young puppy stage—likely already house-trained and out of the most intense chewing/teething period—while still being young and active enough to enjoy adventures with us for many years.

4. **Her photos and videos:** In the photos and short video clip on your website, Luna looks like a gentle, expressive dog. Her body language around volunteers (relaxed posture, wagging tail, soft eyes) suggests that she is social and open to bonding. The video of her taking treats politely and walking nicely on leash really reinforced for us that she would be a good fit for our lifestyle.

5. **Fit with our current pets and lifestyle:** We have a 6-year-old mixed-breed dog and a 4-year-old indoor cat. The notes that Luna has done well in playgroups and that she has been cat-tested and remained curious but not aggressive make us feel that, with careful introductions and management, she could integrate well into our home. Our daily routine (one of us works from home, the other has flexible hours) means Luna would rarely be alone for long stretches, which is helpful for a dog who has recently gone through the stress of losing her previous home.

Overall, we want to adopt Luna because we believe we can offer her a stable, loving, and stimulating environment where she will be treated as a true family member. We are prepared to invest time in continued training, gradual introductions to our existing pets, and helping her decompress and adjust at her own pace. We’re looking not just for "a dog," but for a long-term companion, and based on everything we’ve read and seen, Luna seems like she could be that companion.
about_the_dogcat_you_wish_to_adopt.what_is_your_idea_of_an_ideal_dog_cat_wi:
For a dog, our ideal personality is balanced, affectionate, and adaptable. Specifically, we’re looking for:

- **Friendly and people-oriented:** A dog who enjoys being around people, is happy to greet visitors (after an initial adjustment period), and likes to be involved in family activities. We don’t need a dog who is instantly best friends with everyone, but we do want one who can warm up with gentle handling and positive experiences.

- **Good with other animals:** Because we already have a resident dog and cat, it’s important that the new dog either already has a history of living peacefully with other animals or has the kind of temperament (curious but not pushy, able to read social cues) that suggests they can learn to coexist with proper introductions. We’re looking for a dog who can respect another dog’s space and who is not predatory toward cats.

- **Moderate energy level:** We’re an active family—we walk daily, go on hikes or longer walks on weekends, and enjoy spending time outdoors. Our ideal dog would be enthusiastic about these activities but also able to relax in the house afterward. We’re not looking for a high-intensity working dog who needs hours of intense exercise every single day, but we do want a dog who enjoys playtime, training sessions, and mental enrichment (puzzle toys, sniff walks, etc.).

- **Trainable and eager to please:** We value a dog who is food-motivated or toy-motivated and enjoys learning new things. We don’t expect perfection, but we appreciate a dog who responds well to positive reinforcement, can focus during short training sessions, and is open to new experiences with patient guidance.

- **Resilient but sensitive in a healthy way:** We understand that shelter dogs may have some anxiety or uncertainty at first, and that’s okay. Our ideal dog is one who may be a bit cautious initially but is capable of building confidence with consistent routines and gentle exposure. We prefer a dog who is not extremely reactive or fearful of everyday noises and situations, or who at least shows improvement with support.

For a cat (we are not currently applying for another cat, but for context), our ideal personality is similarly balanced: affectionate but not overly demanding, comfortable living with a dog, playful but not destructive, and able to adjust to a home with a predictable routine. Our current cat Milo fits this description well, and we’d want any future cat to have a temperament that could mesh with that.

In both dogs and cats, we highly value:
- A generally gentle nature
- The ability to relax and settle in the home
- Curiosity without extreme fearfulness
- The capacity to bond with humans and enjoy affection (on their terms)
about_the_dogcat_you_wish_to_adopt.desired_age_and_sex:
We are flexible on sex but have a slight preference for a **female** dog, mainly because our current dog is female and we’ve had good experiences with female-female pairs when personalities are well-matched. That said, we are open to a well-matched male dog if temperament and compatibility are right.

In terms of age, our preferred range is **2 to 6 years old**. We like that dogs in this age range are usually past the very young puppy stage (with its frequent accidents and teething), but still young enough to be active, playful, and adaptable. Luna, at 3 years old, fits perfectly within this range.

We are not currently looking for a senior dog only because our resident dog is already moving into middle age, and we want to stagger ages a bit so that our pets are not all seniors at the same time. However, we are very open to adopting a slightly older dog within that 2–6 range, and we understand that individual health and temperament matter more than the exact number of years.
about_the_dogcat_you_wish_to_adopt.desired_size:
Our desired size for a dog is **medium**, roughly in the **30–55 lb** range. This size works well with our home and yard and is comfortable for us to handle on walks and car trips. We live in a single-family home with a securely fenced backyard, so we have room for a medium dog to move around and play.

We are not looking for a very small dog (toy-sized) because we prefer a dog who can comfortably join us on longer hikes and outdoor activities, and we want a size that feels sturdy around our existing dog. At the same time, we are not currently seeking a very large or giant-breed dog (over ~80 lbs) due to space considerations in our car and the physical strength required for handling.

Luna’s current size (around 40–45 lbs, as listed in her profile) is exactly in the range we’re hoping for, which is one of the reasons she stood out to us.
